---
title: "Why Python?"
---

<span class="time-badge">⏱ 5 minutes</span>


Python is a general-purpose programming language first released in 1991.
It was designed around a simple philosophy: code should be easy to read, easy to write, and easy to share.
Python runs line by line in an interactive environment —
which is why it works so naturally inside *notebooks*, where you can run a single *cell*, inspect
the output, and decide what to do next.
Python is free, open-source, and runs on any operating system.

Over the past decade, Python has become the dominant language for data science and machine learning.
Its ecosystem — `pandas` for data wrangling, `scikit-learn` for machine learning, `matplotlib` and
`seaborn` for visualisation, `PyTorch` and `TensorFlow` for deep learning — is unmatched in breadth
and quality.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-cap: "Python's dominance in data science is reflected in the programming languages used by [2022 Kaggle Machine Learning & Data Science Survey](https://www.kaggle.com/c/kaggle-survey-2022) respondents."
#| fig-alt: "Bar chart showing Python as the most used programming language, followed by SQL and R."

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# Approximate Kaggle usage percentages
data = {
    "Language": ["Python", "SQL", "R", "C++", "MATLAB", "Java", "Bash/Shell"],
    "Usage (%)": [84, 41, 20, 6, 5, 7, 8]
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
bars = plt.bar(df["Language"], df["Usage (%)"], color=[
    "#306998", "#f7952e", "#1f77b4", "#4e79a7",
    "#dd5182", "#ff7f0e", "#2ca02c", "#9467bd", "#8c564b"
])

plt.title("Programming Language Usage (Approx.) — Kaggle Machine Learning & Data Science Survey")
plt.ylabel("Approx. % of Respondents")
plt.ylim(0, 90)

# Add value labels
for i, v in enumerate(df["Usage (%)"]):
    plt.text(i, v + 1, f"{v}%", ha="center", fontweight="bold")

plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### Python by the numbers

The evidence for Python's dominance is unambiguous. After more than a decade of steady growth,
Python's adoption accelerated significantly in 2025, seeing a seven percentage point increase from
2024 — driven by its role as the go-to language for AI, data science, and back-end development,
according to the Stack Overflow Developer Survey, the largest annual survey of the global developer
community. Python is the most desired language among developers who are not currently using it
but want to in the coming year, overtaking JavaScript — a striking signal of where the profession
is heading. Among those learning to code, Python is the most popular language of all,
reflecting how it has become the default entry point into programming for a new generation of
scientists, engineers, and clinicians.

Applied health research fellowships at Oxford, Imperial,
and UCL now routinely specify Python fluency for machine learning work, reflecting an expectation
that serious health data scientists are anchored in Python even when they also use R for statistical
modelling.

---

### Python and the NHS digital transformation

The NHS is in the middle of a substantial digital transformation, and Python has become the de facto language of NHS England's
data science function.

With the legal merger of NHS Digital into NHS England, the new NHS England became one of the largest
digital and technology organisations in the country, directly operating services that patients and
staff rely on every day. The 2025 Medium Term Planning Framework sets out that by 2028/29 all
acute, community and mental health providers will be using the Federated Data Platform (FDP) to
support elective recovery, cancer, and urgent and emergency care. As of May 2025, 91 per cent of
secondary care trusts have an electronic patient record system, generating structured clinical data
at a scale that requires programmatic analysis — not manual spreadsheets.
NHS England publishes its own data science work as open Python code on GitHub, and the
[NHS England Data Science team](https://nhsengland.github.io/datascience/) explicitly positions Python
as its primary language for analytical and machine learning work.

Crucially, NHS England has recognised that clinical staff — not just specialist data scientists —
need these skills. The NHS Clinical Data Science Programme, funded by NHS England since 2023
with growing cohorts in 2024 and 2025, aims to empower healthcare professionals from across the
health and social care workforce to
apply data science in practice and translate data into patient benefit.



---

### What this means for you

The skills you will build in this course — `pandas` for data wrangling, `scikit-learn` for machine
learning, `seaborn` for visualisation — are the skills listed in health data science job
specifications, clinical informatics fellowships, and applied health research postdoctoral
positions. They are also the foundation for everything that comes next: deep learning, natural
language processing of clinical notes, survival analysis, and Bayesian statistical modelling.

That is why we start here.
